chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content:
Seminar. Credit 0 to 1. Reports and discussion of current research and of selected
published technical articles. May not be taken for credit more than once in master's
degree program nor twice in PhD program. Prerequisite: Graduate classification.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Graduate classification.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
The purpose of the course is to expose students to a broad range of current and seminal
research topics in computer science and related fields. Upon completion of the course,
students should be able to prepare reports and discuss research works and selected
published technical articles.

chunks.title: Special Course Designation
chunks.content: None

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
This material Is: Recommended
Houston, We have a narrative
Subtitle: Why science needs story
ISBN: 978-0226270845
Authors: Randy Olson
Publisher: University of Chicago Press

chunks.title: Grading Policy (Overall and Section 600 Reports)
chunks.content:
Publication Date: September 16, 2015
Edition: 1st
To pass this course, you must
1. complete the syllabus quiz on canvas with a grade of 100%, and
2. receive an average score of 80% or higher.
Any course requirements completed before passing the syllabus quiz (i.e., 100% score)
available on Canvas will not be considered.
Section 600:
• You are expected to
• write a report for each of them
• Missed reports without a proper Excused Absence will receive an automatic score
of zero.
• Reports must be typed on a Canvas quiz and require LockDown Browser to avoid
use of external aids (e.g., Al writing tools).
• Report quizzes open at the start of each lecture (12:45pm) and close at the end of
the lecture (2:00pm). We suggest you take notes during the lecture (e.g., on
paper), and then polish the report after the Q&A, up until the quiz closes.
• Each report quiz will include five sections: (1) background, (2) problem statement,
(3) proposed solution, (4) results, and (5) critique. Each section is worth one point.
Thus, to get a score of 80% for each report, you must receive a score of at least 4
out of 5 points.
• Reports submitted without verified attendance will receive a score of zero.

chunks.title: Section 600 Attendance and Verification
chunks.content:
Section 600:
• You are expected to
• attend all the lectures scheduled during the semester, and
• You must bring your TAMU ID to the seminar so we can verify you attended the
lecture.
• Other forms of identification (e.g., driver's license) will NOT be accepted.
• Please come to the seminar room 5-10 minutes early so we can scan your
ID.
• If you arrive late (2+ minutes after the start time), you can still attend the
lecture but your ID will not be scanned and your attendance will not be
verified.

chunks.title: Section 700 Grading
chunks.content:
Section 700:
• You are expected to
• watch the recordings of all the lectures scheduled during the semester, and
• write a report for each of them
• Lecture recordings will be available on Canvas, typically within 2-4 hours after the
lecture ends. You will be able to watch the lecture recording at your leisure.
• Report quizzes will also be available on Canvas, typically within 24 hours after the
lecture ends. Report quizzes require LockDown Browser and are time limited (<15
minutes), so be sure to watch the recording in advance and take notes.
• Report quizzes must be submitted on Canvas by the end of the week (i.e., Sunday
midnight).
• Each report quiz will consist of a series of multiple-choice, multiple-answer
questions related to the contents of the lecture. The number of questions may vary
from lecture to lecture depending on the contents of the lecture. The value of each
quiz question (i.e., number of points) will vary depending on the complexity of the
question, and the total score will be normalized to a 5-point scale.
Final grade. Your final grade will be the statistical mean of the top 75% of the individual
quiz grades, i.e., the lowest 25% of your quiz grades do not count, regardless of whether
you completed them or not. This gives you a bit of flexibility: a few low quiz grades may
not affect your grade, and you may miss a few unexcused lectures (e.g., you arrived late
to class, your battery died, connection issues).
Example: Assume we have 12 lectures during the semester (the actual number is
likely to be higher). Further, assume you received the following scores on the 12
lectures: [5.0, 4.5, 4.5, 4, 4, 4, 4, 3.5, 3.0, 2.0, 0.0, 0.0]. Your final score will be 4.05,
which is the average of the top 75% of your grades (i.e., we dropped the lowest 3
scores.)

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not
considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Late work is not accepted under any circumstances. Please review the grading policy.

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
The course schedule is available as a spreadsheet. Please review periodically as new
speakers are added throughout the semester.

chunks.title: ATTENDANCE AND ETIQUETTE (SECTION 600 ONLY)
chunks.content:
Please reserve the entire scheduled time slot, and plan to arrive at least 10 minutes
before the start of the talk, since we will be taking class attendance. For this purpose, you
are required to have your Aggie ID card with you.
During the seminar, you are expected to pay attention and refrain from activities such as
doing other work, reading the newspaper, surfing the web, or sleeping. Behavior such as
this gives speakers a bad impression of our graduate program and students. Students
noticed exhibiting such behavior will not receive credit for attending that seminar.
In some cases, the seminars will finish a few minutes early. If the speaker is not finished,
or if the Q&A period is ongoing, you are expected to stay until the end of the talk.

chunks.title: EXCUSED ABSENCES
chunks.content:
The grading criteria (we drop the bottom 25% of your report grades) avoids much of the
need to request excused absences. If you want to request an excused absence, please
follow these steps:
• Go to Student Rule 7 to determine if your situation is covered, then
• Send an email to csce-681-rgo@tamu.edu with:
• A description of your situation, and
• Which provision within Student Rule 7 applies
• Supporting documentation (doctor's note, cover letter, etc.)
• Your request will be reviewed, and you will be notified of its approval/rejection via
email
Timeline for submitting excused absences: Per Student Rule 7, you must make the
request prior to the day of absence. If this is not possible, you must provide notification
by the end of the second business day after the last date of the absence, including an
explanation of why advance notice could not be sent. Requests for excused absences
that do not meet these guidelines will not be considered. Missed lectures with an approved excused absence will not be counted towards the final
grade. Missed lectures without an approved excused absence will receive an automated
score of zero.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content:
Seminar reports containing plagiarized content (including self-plagiarism or use of any
machine-generated text or translation tools) will be submitted to the Aggie Honors Office,
with the recommendation that the student receives an F* for the course.. Please review
the TAMU guidelines for Academic Integrity and Plagiarism.
Occasionally, we find evidence of plagiarism after the reports have been graded. For
example, a student may submit a report that has been plagiarized from a prior
submission, or a prior report will be flagged when a new detector of Al-generated text
becomes available. For these reasons, you should treat grades for seminar reports as
provisional. Any prior submission that is later found to constitute a violation will be
submitted to the Aggie Honors Office, with the same recommendation that the student
receives an F*.
Any information (e.g., a doctor's note for an excused absences) that is found to have
been fabricated, falsified and/or misused will be submitted to the Aggie Honors Office
with the recommendation the student receives an F* for the course.

chunks.title: Course Specific Makeup Work Policy
chunks.content:
Makeup work will not be allowed given that the grading policy provides flexibility for
missed lectures and/or poor grades on certain report quizzes.

chunks.title: Statement on Mental Health and Wellness
chunks.content:
Texas A&M University recognizes that mental health and wellness are critical
factors influencing a student's academic success and overall wellbeing. Students
are encouraged to engage in healthy self-care practices by utilizing the resources
and services available through University Health Services. The TELUS Health
Student Support app provides access to professional counseling in multiple
languages anytime, anywhere by phone or chat, and the 988 Suicide & Crisis
Lifeline offers 24-hour emergency support at 988 or 988lifeline.org.
Texas A&M College Station
Students needing a listening ear can contact University Health Services at
979.458.4584. Call 911 or visit your nearest emergency room if you are currently
experiencing a life-threatening situation or if your safety is at risk. 24-hour
emergency help is also available through the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline (988) or
at 988lifeline.org.
